skill.name: azure-ai-contentsafety-py
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure AI Content Safety SDK for Python to classify harmful text and images, manage blocklists, and tune severity levels.
skill.summary: Use Azure AI Content Safety from Python to analyze text, images, harm categories, severity levels, and custom blocklists.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-cryptography findings were false positives caused by markdown fences, tables, and descriptive text. The confirmed risk is legitimate but material: the examples read Azure credentials, read image files, and send text or image content to Azure AI Content Safety endpoints.

medium_findings.title: External Azure Content Safety Calls
medium_findings.description: The skill demonstrates sending text, image data, image URLs, and blocklist data to Azure AI Content Safety clients. This is expected for the SDK, but users may transmit sensitive content to an external cloud service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SDK calls are explicit and semantically match the skill purpose. The risk is data exposure to Azure, not malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Endpoint Access in Examples
medium_findings.description: The authentication examples read CONTENT_SAFETY_ENDPOINT and CONTENT_SAFETY_KEY from environment variables. This is a standard pattern, but it requires careful secret handling and should not be logged or copied into prompts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable reads are direct and relevant to authentication. The usage appears legitimate because credentials are passed to Azure SDK clients only.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are markdown code fences and inline documentation markers, not executable Ruby or shell code. No evidence found of instructions that run arbitrary shell commands beyond a package installation example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows markdown fences, Python snippets, and tables at the reported locations. There is no dynamic command execution path in the single reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Documentation Placeholders
low_findings.description: The endpoint and image URL examples use placeholders for an Azure resource endpoint and sample image URL. They are not hidden destinations or unauthorized exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URLs are visible documentation examples. One uses an Azure resource placeholder and the other uses example.com.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines contain skill metadata, a blocklist description string, and a harm-category table header. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are not cryptographic operations. The scanner appears to have matched unrelated text.

low_findings.title: Local Image File Read Example
low_findings.description: The image analysis example reads image.jpg from the local filesystem and encodes it for analysis. This is normal SDK usage, but users should avoid uploading private images without consent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file read is explicit and limited to a named image example. The risk depends on user-selected files rather than hidden behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Submission of User Content
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples call analyze_text and analyze_image on Azure SDK clients, which submits text or image content to the configured Azure service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The calls and request objects are visible and match the SDK workflow. This is expected functionality with privacy implications.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment-Based Secret Access
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples load an endpoint and API key from environment variables for Azure SDK authentication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly references os.environ for Azure configuration. The pattern is legitimate but still involves credentials.

content.user_title: Moderate Content with Azure AI Safety
content.value_statement: Unsafe user content can expose applications to policy and trust risks. This skill guides Python integration with Azure AI Content Safety for text, images, severity levels, and blocklists.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install the azure-ai-contentsafety Python package.
Configures ContentSafetyClient with an API key or DefaultAzureCredential.
Analyzes text for hate, sexual, violence, and self-harm categories.
Analyzes images from local files or image URLs.
Creates and updates text blocklists and blocklist items.
Explains severity levels, harm categories, and client types.
limitations: Requires an Azure AI Content Safety resource, endpoint, and credential.
Provides snippets, not a complete moderation service or user interface.
Does not define privacy, retention, or compliance controls for submitted content.
Does not choose severity thresholds for every product or jurisdiction.

use_cases.title: Screen User Posts
use_cases.description: Add Python moderation checks before publishing user-generated text or images.

use_cases.title: Review AI Outputs
use_cases.description: Classify generated responses before they are displayed to users.

use_cases.title: Manage Custom Blocklists
use_cases.description: Create blocklists for product-specific terms and enforce immediate rejection rules.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up the Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure Azure AI Content Safety in a Python app using environment variables and an API key.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Text Severity
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to analyze a text string for harm categories and explain how to interpret each severity score.

prompt_templates.title: Moderate Images
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through analyzing an uploaded image and an image URL with Azure AI Content Safety in Python.

prompt_templates.title: Design Blocklist Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Python moderation workflow that combines severity thresholds, custom blocklists, and audit logging.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to check a user comment before publication.
output_examples.output: The answer outlines client setup, text analysis, category review, threshold checks, and a publish or reject decision.

output_examples.input: A trust and safety lead asks how custom blocked terms work.
output_examples.output: The answer explains creating a blocklist, adding terms, enabling halt-on-match behavior, and reviewing matched items.

output_examples.input: An AI team asks how to moderate generated images.
output_examples.output: The answer describes preparing image data, submitting it to the image analysis client, and mapping severity levels to product actions.

best_practices: Keep Azure keys in secret storage or environment variables, and never paste them into prompts.
Document which content is sent to Azure before enabling moderation in production.
Choose thresholds with policy owners, then log outcomes for review and tuning.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode Azure keys or resource endpoints in application source files.
Do not upload private images or messages without a valid product and privacy basis.
Do not treat a single severity score as a complete policy decision.

faq.question: Does this skill run moderation by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides Python SDK guidance and examples that developers apply in their own applications.

faq.question: What Azure credentials are needed?
faq.answer: The examples use an Azure Content Safety endpoint with either an API key or DefaultAzureCredential.

faq.question: Can it analyze both text and images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for text analysis, local image analysis, and image URL analysis.

faq.question: Does it support custom blocklists?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to create blocklists, add items, and analyze text with blocklist matching enabled.

faq.question: Is content sent to an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. The SDK examples send selected text or image data to the configured Azure Content Safety resource.

faq.question: Does it define the right moderation threshold?
faq.answer: No. It explains severity levels, but each product team must choose thresholds for its policy and users.
